turns.content: Why does a persistent segment tree even work this way when I just need to update values in place? It feels like overkill for our production logs.

You're right, standard updates break the history chain. A versioned segment tree creates new nodes only on change paths while reusing old nodes elsewhere. In Rust you implement this with a Vec<Node> and an index map where each Node holds left_child_idx and right_child_idx. This ensures O(log n) update time because you traverse height h = log2(n) levels, creating exactly 2h+1 new nodes per operation.
So I'm not modifying existing allocations? That sounds expensive for memory management in a high-throughput system.

Exactly, you avoid mutating the tree structure. Instead of changing pointers in place, you allocate fresh Node instances and link them to the old root via an index array. Rust's Box<Node> handles ownership cleanly here since each version is a distinct heap allocation. The Vec<Node> grows linearly with versions while the index map stays constant size relative to n.
Can I query ranges across different versions efficiently without rebuilding everything?

Yes, you traverse the version-specific root using the same segment tree logic. For a range sum query on version v, you start at root[v] and recursively aggregate sums from left_child_idx and right_child_idx until base cases hit. This works because each version's path is pre-built during its creation. In Rust you'd store Vec<RootIndex> where RootIndex points into the shared Node pool.
What about handling deletions or point updates without creating too many nodes?

Point updates create only O(log n) new nodes since you only modify the path to that leaf. Deletions are trickier—you replace a node value with -infinity and mark it as deleted in a separate status field within Node. You can implement this with Option<Node> or a flag inside Node to avoid allocating garbage later.
Does Rust's borrow checker make this harder than C++?

Not really, as long as you keep the index map immutable once created. Since Node instances are owned via Box and accessed through indices, there's no borrowing conflict between versions. You just need to ensure your Vec<Node> and RootIndex don't mutate after initialization. The borrow checker actually helps prevent accidental cross-version corruption.
I tried implementing this but my segment tree queries are returning wrong sums for older versions.

Check if your index map is being updated correctly during version creation. When you insert a new version, the old root must remain untouched in memory while you build the new path to it. If you're accidentally modifying shared nodes before finalizing the new version, your queries will see inconsistent data. Use temporary vectors for intermediate Node allocations until all paths are stable.
Okay, so I need to isolate each version's node creation phase completely?

Precisely. Think of it as snapshotting the entire subtree structure before any mutations. In Rust you can use Arc<Node> for shared immutable parts if needed, but a simple Vec with indices is often sufficient and faster. Once all new nodes are allocated, link them to their parents in the index map, then swap roots atomically.

Hey, I'm working on a proof-of-concept persistent segment tree in Rust. The goal is to support O(log n) updates and sharing nodes between versions efficiently. I've got this code snippet, but it's not quite working right:

That sounds interesting! Persistent data structures like the persistent segment tree are tricky — especially when you want efficient updates and node sharing between states. Can you share your current code? I can help identify what might be going wrong.
Sure, here's a simplified version:
Great! Let me take a look. So you've got something like this?
Yeah, basically. The Node struct has left and right children, and I'm cloning nodes on update to preserve history.

Ah, so you're trying to implement a persistent segment tree with versioning by copying node structs. That's good because it allows efficient sharing of old versions while enabling modifications without mutation side effects. However, there’s a subtle issue: if the nodes are not properly shared across versions, updates can become expensive or even incorrect.
Hmm, I see. Maybe that's it? So what should I do instead?

Instead of cloning whole subtrees, you want to share as much node state as possible between versions while updating only the necessary parts. This is similar to how persistent data structures like persistent vectors or trees are typically implemented.
Wait, so I should avoid deep copying? But then won't updates get messy?
Exactly! The trick is to update only the leaf node and its ancestors while sharing unchanged subtrees. This way, you keep O(log n) update complexity.
Right, so I should clone just the path from root to the updated node?

Yes! That’s correct. Imagine a versioned segment tree as a forest of trees at different versions — you want each new version to reference previous nodes wherever possible except for the modified parts.
Okay, but how do I handle lookups in such a setup?

Good question. At lookup time, you can traverse each version tree simultaneously — comparing the current version's node with the previous one to determine if it was updated or not. This is analogous to merging two persistent trees.
That makes sense! So I'll need a reference to a parent version for each node?
Yes, that’s essential. Each `Node` should have pointers back to its parent versions so you can traverse the tree and merge states efficiently.
Alright, thanks! I'll try updating my implementation accordingly.
You're welcome! And remember — persistent data structures are tricky but powerful. If you hit any more issues or need further clarifications, feel free to ask.
